title: Budget Update
text: Update on 2024-25 Budget (July 2, 2024); Additional Information; What are core funds? What are non-core funds?; Core funds make up approximately 55% of our campus resources and are primarily sourced from state funds, tuition, certain fees, indirect cost recovery, and investment income. These resources are intended to support the university’s core mission.; Non-core funds make up the other half of our campus resources and are primarily sourced from auxiliary units, such as housing and dining, grants, contracts, awards, and philanthropy. Core funds make up a larger proportion of UC Santa Cruz’s total budget than most of the other UC campuses.; The Campus Deficit; Similar to other University of California campuses, over the past few years UC Santa Cruz has developed a growing structural deficit in core funds. This deficit is the result of annual expenses increasingly outpacing our annual revenues.

title: Budget Update
text: UC Santa Cruz leaders are working to develop a multi-year plan to address the structural deficit, which would otherwise continue to grow as costs increase and revenue remains mostly steady.; Take a few minutes to learn about the UC Santa Cruz budget and our core funds challenges.; What’s driving the core fund structural deficit?; UC Santa Cruz’s structural deficit developed in recent years and is growing due to a combination of factors.; Salaries and benefits. About 80% of our operating expenditures are related to employee compensation — our faculty and staff are essential to our core mission. Salaries increase annually due to collective bargaining agreements, merit-based raises, experience-based step increases, and other factors. Similarly, UC benefits costs — such as health insurance — are projected to increase by an additional 16.4% across FY24 and FY25.; Enrollment growth has been constrained for several years due to the limited housing availability on campus and in our community.

title: Budget Update
text: Enrollment of international and out-of-state students (who pay higher tuition rates) declined during the pandemic mitigations and may not regain the same level for several years. These realities impact our revenue streams. Other mandatory costs. Our campus is also experiencing other cost increases, such as utilities, insurance and more. Natural disasters, such as fires and floods, are driving up insurance costs across the state. Similarly, utility costs are rising significantly. In addition to the cost of lighting, heating, and powering our classrooms, offices, and residence halls, many of our scientific activities require ongoing energy use. It is important to understand that UC Santa Cruz uses energy efficiently. Actual energy use has decreased while our total square footage has grown. The campus’s sustainability efforts have played a key role in mitigating the impact of these rising utility costs.; What’s the current status of UC Santa Cruz’s core funds deficit?;

title: Budget Update
text: A structural deficit occurs when annual expenses consistently exceed annual revenue, resulting in a recurring shortfall. This gap requires us to draw down on our reserves, something that is unsustainable, and will hinder us from being able to borrow in order to finance future capital projects. ; UC Santa Cruz has experienced structural deficits from time to time (early-mid 1990s, during the great recession, etc.) and we have used one-time savings combined with budget reduction strategies to recover. The current structural deficit in core funds began in 2020 and has escalated quickly.

title: Budget Update
text: What is different this time is that (1) many services initiated using one-time federal Higher Education Emergency Relief Funds (HEERF), during the pandemic, are of an ongoing nature such as those necessary to support student success, remote work, and mitigate increasing cyber threat, (2) total employee costs (including benefits) are increasing at a faster pace than revenues, and (3) our reserves have been depleted and are no longer available to bridge the deficit.; The estimated structural deficit in our core funds has been growing more significantly since 2023. ; The 2024-25 budget anticipates revenue for UC Santa Cruz at just over $1 billion, of which approximately $556 million is in core funds. The projected annual expenditures in core funds total $667 million, resulting in a FY25 core funds budget gap of -$111 million. ; How does the state budget shortfall affect UC Santa Cruz? ;

title: Budget Update
text: The Governor’s FY25 Budget for the State of California remained generally committed to the multi-year funding compact with the UC (+5% general fund increase), even in the midst of a $46.8 billion anticipated state budget shortfall . This was a positive development in the budget, resulting in an estimated $8.2 million in additional funding to our campus. While many state agencies took close to 8% reductions this year, the Governor has signaled the UC will receive a 7.9% reduction in FY26, along with deferral of the funding compact to FY27. ; Preliminary estimates provided by UCOP indicate we should anticipate a $16.6 million budget reduction in FY26, which will increase our budget deficit next year. It is important to note that salary and benefit costs will continue to increase even with this anticipated reduction in state funding, which will further exacerbate our structural deficit issues.

title: Budget Update
text: We are actively working to incorporate these planning assumptions into refined multi-year projections which will inform multi-year reduction plans under development this fall.; How are we planning to address the situation?; The campus is actively taking steps to close the structural deficit gap so that we can sustainably support the campus mission into the future:; Reductions totaling $17 million were implemented as of July 1, 2024.; Principal officers continue to reduce spending in the current fiscal year by placing more scrutiny on core funds discretionary spending, such as hiring, contracting, travel, etc.; Vice Chancellor for Finance, Operations and Administration Ed Reiskin and Vice Provost for Academic Affairs Herbie Lee are leading a budget advisory committee to make multi-year reduction plan recommendations to the chancellor and campus provost and executive vice chancellor.;

title: Budget Update
text: A committee led by Vice Chancellor for Student Affairs and Success Akirah Bradley-Armstrong and Associate Vice Chancellor for Financial Affairs Biju Kamaleswaran has been established and charged with developing ideas to increase revenues and soliciting and reviewing suggestions from the entire campus community.; These FAQ were collected and answered in October 2024; Why did the campus only begin to address the structural deficit last academic year?; How is the campus working to address the structural core funds deficit?; How is the campus exploring re-organizations or realignments?; How will the campus plan to adapt workloads as organizations evolve?; How will the STAR program be impacted by budget constraints?; What measures are being implemented to increase non-resident enrollment and summer enrollment to pre-pandemic levels?; Is the campus exploring any new financial tools to help with more effective budgeting/tracking, either in the near or long term?;

title: Budget Update
text: Why is UC Santa Cruz more impacted than other UC campuses?; Is UCSC considering implementing staff furloughs?; What tools are available to help me manage my campus budget?; How can staff and faculty provide input and ideas throughout this situation?; How can I learn more and stay informed?; UC Santa Cruz had experienced a relatively small, ongoing structural deficit in the past but we had been able to use one-time savings combined with budget reduction strategies to manage it. ; The campus has been working to implement budget models and practices that both provide better forecasting and allow unused allocations to be returned, such as the Academic Budget Resource model that was developed in 2022.; The current structural deficit in core funds began in 2020 as the campus worked to protect jobs during the pandemic. The deficit rapidly escalated with a confluence of cost increases across many different areas in 2023.

title: Budget Update
text: The campus began taking more concerted action in 2023 and in early 2024 began working with divisional leaders to reduce discretionary spending and slow hiring. ; How is the campus working to address the structural core funds deficit? ; UC Santa Cruz is working hard to ensure long-term financial sustainability for the campus and we recognize our role as an anchor employer in the region. UC Santa Cruz is focusing both on increasing revenues and reducing expenses. ; We are a people-first organization, and every effort is being made to mitigate the need to reduce filled positions. Every organization manages the size and composition of its workforce, and we anticipate that, like in all organizations our size, there will continue to be new hires, reorganizations, and separations for a range of operational reasons. Staff Human Resources provides a wealth of information for employees and managers about employee benefits and support. ; The campus has implemented “position management” to limit hiring to only the most critical positions. ;

title: Budget Update
text: Only three faculty positions were authorized for the 2024-25 hiring cycle and faculty hiring will continue to be limited to only the most critical positions.; All discretionary spending has been targeted for reduction, including travel, entertainment and supplies, and limitations on this type of spending have been implemented across the divisions. ; The benefits model changed to give division leaders and hiring managers a better understanding of the full cost of each employee. Under this new benefits model, benefits costs will no longer be held centrally, but rather will be allocated out to division budgets, just as salary costs are, requiring units to account for the cost of benefits for all positions. ; Principal Officers submitted expenditure reduction scenarios that were reviewed by the Budget Advisory Committee (BAC) resulting in savings of almost $17 million. Additional savings targets for FY 25 will result in savings of around $13 million.

title: Budget Update
text: Academic divisions received lower targets, whereas administrative divisions received higher targets, in an effort to minimize the impact to units directly connected to our core mission.; Carryforward spending authority has been reduced in each division to help return nearly 50 percent of unrestricted carryforward. ; The Budget Advisory Committee has recommended a multi-year budget balancing framework to chart a path to fiscal sustainability.; Multiple campus committees are working collaboratively on ideas around revenue generation, restructuring, and efficiency to improve our financial position while maintaining our work to support our teaching and research mission.; Campus leadership is working collaboratively to develop plans to continue progress toward campus goals while also reorganizing or realigning areas. The primary driver in considering reorganizations is to maintain and enhance critical functions to meet the campus’s core mission. Teaching, learning, and research are core to the campus mission and the campus will remain a high-impact university committed to student success.

title: Budget Update
text: Multiple campus committees are collaborating on ideas around revenue generation, restructuring, and efficiency to improve our financial position while maintaining our work to support our teaching and research mission.; Campus leadership has developed Principles for Adapting Workloads during Budget Realignment to support staff, maximize efficiency, and help us continue to meet our campus mission as we adapt our workloads and work collaboratively across units.; How will the STAR program be impacted by budget constraints? ; Due to budget constraints, the campus made the difficult decision to pause the program for the 2024-25 fiscal year. UC Santa Cruz remains committed to retaining a talented workforce, recognizing high-performing individuals, and recruiting for critical positions.; During and after the pandemic, UC Santa Cruz experienced a sharp decline in enrollment of international and out-of-state students. Students from other countries and states enrich our campus community and are exploring opportunities to restore international and non-resident enrollment to pre-pandemic levels.

title: Budget Update
text: Summer enrollment has more than rebounded, thanks to a number of deliberate strategies to attract more students.; Yes, the campus is actively developing several new financial tools to help with budgeting and tracking, including: ; a five-year interactive budget planning model to support proactive planning and improved understanding of the financial impacts of budget decisions over a multi-year planning horizon; ; quarterly reports/projections to monitor year-to date performance, flag potential issues as they arise, and project remaining resources available at year end; and; monthly tracking to monitor progress on spending curtailment and other reduction measures.; In addition, the campus is exploring an enterprise budgeting system to enhance and automate budget processes, support greater transparency and visibility, and improve planning, forecasting, reporting, and decision making.; Seven of the nine undergraduate-serving UC campuses have been public about managing a structural deficit.

title: Budget Update
text: UC Santa Cruz does not have a medical school or professional schools that return funds to campus cover some core-funded expenses. ; Enrollment growth, through state support and tuition, also provides additional financial resources for campuses. Responsive to the state’s need to provide educational opportunities for California’s students, UC Santa Cruz is committed to increasing educational access and has a holistic framework to guide future physical planning on the campus with its 2021 Long Range Development Plan. The campus has held enrollment relatively flat while additional student housing is built. Delays to housing development not only increase the cost of the housing, but prevent growth that could support our budget. UC Santa Cruz has also recovered more slowly from a decline in international student enrollment during the pandemic.; Not at this time. While furloughs can be a tool to address a discrete one-time budget challenge, they are not the first choice in solving a structural challenge and would not create permanent cost savings. ;

title: Budget Update
text: The Budget Analysis and Planning Office has created Resources and Guidelines for Budget Management to help you and your team navigate the current budget environment.; UC Santa Cruz is soliciting ideas from staff and faculty for revenue growth and operational efficiency. Please visit https://foa.ucsc.edu/revenue-generation/your-ideas-matter/ to submit your ideas!; How can I learn more and stay informed? ; UC Santa Cruz will continue to share updates in various ways, including posts on the budget website, employee town halls, and campus messages. ; Last modified: Jun 08, 2026;

title: Building Maintenance (Campus Facilities)
text: PPDO light themed logo; Physical Planning, Development & Operations; HomeFacilities Management; Aerial View with parts of UCSC campus, redwood trees, and a hazy coastline.; Energy Management; Facilities Management ensures that UCSC buildings and equipment operate efficiently, sustainably, and safely by optimizing energy usage, maintaining campus facilities, and managing space utilization. The collective efforts of our three teams support a functional, environmentally conscious, and well-maintained campus environment for the university community.; Units; Plant technician working on equipment; Physical Plant is dedicated to maintaining and improving the campus environment to promote academic, research, and community success. Our teams focus on ensuring the cleanliness, safety, and smooth operation of university facilities, infrastructure, and vehicles. We take pride in managing the university’s fleet to support transportation needs while providing dependable services that contribute to a welcoming, sustainable, and efficient campus for students, faculty, staff, and visitors.;

title: Building Maintenance (Campus Facilities)
text: UCSC Fleet Services Truck; Fleet Services will provide high-quality, efficient, and cost effective services for the over 700 vehicles and ancillary equipment operated at UCSC. We further pledge to offer sustainable transportation that is responsive to the needs of the campus community and supports the mission of the University of California. For over 40 years Fleet Services has kept UCSC moving. We offer daily rentals for staff and student groups, vehicle lease-to-own arrangements, and advice to departments looking to purchase and operate a vehicle at the University.; Aerial view of the east remote lot photovoltaic array; Energy Management focuses on reducing campus energy consumption and greenhouse gas emissions through tracking and analysis. We oversee energy metering systems, handle utility data, and support initiatives for energy efficiency, including renewable energy projects on-site.

title: Campus and Community Planning
text: Click here to RSVP to the Design + Construction Industry Outreach Event on 8/10/26!; Physical Planning, Development & Operations (PPDO) integrates six core campus departments: Planning Services, Design & Construction, Work Management, Physical Plant Services, Space Management, and Energy Management. These departments have been strategically consolidated into three primary units: Planning, Design & Construction; Facilities Management; and the Business Office. This unified structure streamlines campus planning and operational services. PPDO is a department within the Division of Finance, Operations and Administration (FOA).; Learn more about PPDO; top down view of a building in the forest; The Planning, Design & Construction unit guides development initiatives while balancing infrastructure needs with environmental stewardship and community integration. The unit ensures all projects comply with the Long Range Development Plan and key environmental regulations, providing critical oversight of the university’s physical growth.

title: Campus and Community Planning
text: The Planning, Design & Construction unit manages capital construction and maintenance projects with a focus on creating sustainable and functional facilities. Through collaborative partnerships across campus and with local agencies, the department maintains excellence in design while preserving UCSC’s unique natural environment and meeting community needs.; Learn more about Planning, Design & Construction; students standing and talking in a building hallway; The university’s Facilities Management unit operates through three interconnected departments to maintain and optimize campus operations. Physical Plant Services handles essential maintenance and fleet management, ensuring the campus remains clean, safe, and functional for all community members. Space Management oversees the allocation and tracking of campus facilities, maintaining comprehensive databases and providing strategic insights for space utilization and planning. The Energy Management team leads sustainability efforts by monitoring utility usage, implementing energy-efficient solutions, and advancing the campus’s environmental goals through innovative technologies and practices.; Learn more about Facilities Management; Students walking on a bridge towards Kresge College;

title: Campus and Community Planning
text: The Business Office unit provides the back-office and administrative support for all PPDO departments. Among its responsibilities are executing the bidding and contracting process for construction projects, administering department disbursement and procurement, running the operations support group, administering local recharge financial processes, and administering the archival construction data systems.; Learn more about the Business Services; Planning, Design & Construction Services; start a project icon; To start any facility or infrastructure-related improvement project, the Primary Project Contact or Principal Officer for the Division must initiate a Start-A-Project Request.; arithmetic operations; A cost estimate is an accurate accounting of expected project costs. title: Campus Counsel
text: UCSC Office of Campus Counsel; Aerial view of Monterey Bay from UCSC Campus; Welcome to UCSC Office of Campus Counsel!; About us:; Our office supports the mission of the UC Santa Cruz campus. UCSC attorneys provide legal advice, guidance and assistance on a wide range of matters affecting UC Santa Cruz including academic and student affairs, business transactions, labor and employment, research, compliance, and other issues related to University business and operations.; Our attorneys and legal operations staff work collaboratively as trusted partners to deliver timely and responsive solutions, resolve legal disputes, and advocate on behalf of the campus. The Santa Cruz Office of Campus Counsel is part of UC Legal – Office of the General Counsel. UCSC attorneys represent the University as an institution, and are not able to provide legal advice to faculty, staff or students in their individual capacity. ; Contact Us:; E-Mail: campuscounsel@ucsc.edu;

title: Campus Life
text: Campus elections are one of the most powerful ways that Banana Slugs can shape the future of UCSC and participate in civic engagement. Whether you’re thinking about running for a leadership position in the undergraduate Student Union Assembly (SUA) or the Graduate Student Association (GSA), voting on critical student fee measures, or simply want to learn more about the campus elections process- this is your hub for up-to-date information. This page features important dates, deadlines, candidate details, ballot initiatives, campus elections archives with the outcomes of previous elections, and other key information to help you participate fully in the democratic process. Campus elections at UCSC reflect student voice and deliver meaningful change that directly shapes the student experience and the campus community. Get informed, get involved, and make it count.; Learn about campus elections; Students participating in student elections.; Check out the Dean of Students Instagram;

title: Campus Portrait
text: HomeUCSC at a GlanceCampus Portrait; The UC Santa Cruz Campus Portrait is a dashboard that displays key institutional data and accountability statistics, primarily about undergraduate students and their educational outcomes. The model for the UC version of the portrait was the Voluntary System of Accountability’s (VSA) “College Portrait.” This display of institutional data in a consistent format for use by potential applicants and parents was developed by the American Association of Schools and Colleges and Universities (AASCU) and the National Association of State Universities and Land Grant Colleges (NASULGC).; If you experience any accessibility barriers with this data visualization, please contact iraps@ucsc.edu. ; Last modified: Apr 16, 2026;

title: Campus Sustainability
text: Leading in Sustainability; We’re collecting feedback on our Sustainability & Climate Action Plan!; Please submit your thoughts and ideas here.; Explore Our Impact; Discover Our Initiatives; Explore our Plan; Accomplishments; Learn About Our Accomplishments; 2025 Goals and Progress; Campus water conservation has surpassed its 2025 target. The university has already reduced water usage by 41%, exceeding the original goal of a 36% cut compared to 2005–08 levels.; Water reduction vs 2005–08 baseline; 41% vs 36% target · 114% of goal; Waste; Significant progress has been made toward reducing and diverting waste, though some goals are still ahead. Per capita municipal solid waste generation is already down 25.28%, meeting the 2025 target, and the campus has reached 49% diversion from landfill toward the 90% goal set for 2030. The initiative to eliminate single-use plastics in vending machines is 90% complete.; Waste per-capita reduction;

title: Cantú Queer Center
text: Sammy slug holding pride flag; A cozy space for queer connection; Mission and vision; The Lionel Cantú Queer Resource Center co-creates experiences with and for students, staff, and faculty to challenge cisheteronormative understandings of gender and sexuality. We build a more inclusive campus community by providing resources, care, advocacy, and education that disrupts binaries and honors the intersecting realities and dynamics within gender and sexual diversities. We envision communities where people of all genders and sexualities are able to thrive and celebrate their whole personhood. ; Visit the Cantú Cabin; Tucked behind Merrill and Crown Colleges, the Cantú Queer Center is a cozy, treehouse-like space where students can connect, feel affirmed, and find support. Whether you’re stopping by to attend an event, ask questions, or just rest between classes, the Cabin offers resources, care, advocacy, and education in a welcoming environment.;

title: Cantú Queer Center
text: Find your way to the Cantú Cabin; Cantu queer center cabin; All-gender restroom locations; All-gender restrooms are available throughout the UCSC campus, providing inclusive facilities for everyone in our community. These restrooms are especially important for transgender and nonbinary individuals—and thanks to the continued advocacy of students, staff, and the Cantú Queer Center, access to all-gender restrooms is growing across campus.; Find all-gender restrooms on campus; Screenshot of all gender restroom locations; Explore the Cantú Clothing Closet; Find (free!) clothing and accessories that support your gender expression in a welcoming space. The Cantú Clothing Closet offers everything from everyday wear to gender-affirming items like chest binders, tucking underwear, and more. It’s here for trans, gender-nonconforming, and queer students who want to try out new styles without the pressure of traditional shopping.;

title: Cantú Queer Center
text: Looking for binders, packers, or trans tape? Visit our Gender Affirming Gear (GAG) Closet page to learn more.; Cantu center clothing closet; Your safety matters; If you or someone you know has experienced harassment or discrimination, you have support. The Office for Equity and Equal Protection (EEP) handles all complaints of discrimination or harassment on the basis of protected characteristics (such as race, disability, sex, gender, religion, and age) for all community members, including students. You can submit a report online at any time.; Access the online reporting form; Cherry blossom tree; Follow us on Instagram!; The Lionel Cantu Queer Center is part of a network of campus resource centers that work together to support all students.

title: Cantú Queer Center
text: In collaboration with centers like AARCC, AIRC, El Centro, AA/PIRC, and Women’s Center, we offer shared programs, resources, and spaces that celebrate identity, build community, and promote belonging. In addition to exploring our offerings, we encourage you to check out the many events, services, and opportunities provided by our partner centers across campus. UCSC resource centers are open to all students, and everyone is welcome.;

title: Capital Planning and Space Management (Planning and Budget)
text: HomeCampus PlanningCapital and Space Planning; stationery items in a desktop organizer; Space Planning Guidelines; Policies & Standards; Space Request or Release; Occuspace at UCSC; icon of a person with a hardhat ; Under the leadership of the Associate Vice Chancellor for Physical Planning, Development and Operations (PPDO), Capital and Space Planning (CaSP) plans capital projects and campus spaces in support of students, faculty, staff, and academic and administrative units.; Capital Planning includes long-term physical assets such as land, buildings, and major infrastructure. CaSP works with campus stakeholders from the earliest project concepts through programming and financial feasibility, and prepares the annual Consolidated Capital Report, which includes the six-year Capital Financial Plan (CFP) for Regents’ acceptance.;

title: Capital Planning and Space Management (Planning and Budget)
text: Space Planning coordinates with campus administration and academic units to optimize the use of UCSC facilities in support of the university’s teaching, research, and public service mission. Space Planning also makes recommendations to campus leadership to ensure space use aligns with institutional priorities and strategic goals.; Capital and Space Planning and Projects; Capital Planning process; Capital planning is an iterative process that supports campus growth and renewal through investments in buildings, infrastructure, and land. At UCSC, the process considers proposals for new projects alongside the condition and performance of existing facilities and systems. Project priorities are evaluated using a range of parameters, including current and projected enrollment, space utilization, student contact hours, and employment levels. These factors are reviewed in the context of the campus mission, long-range development plan, budget forecasts, and stakeholder input. Capital planning outcomes are reflected in the Consolidated Capital Report published each year.;

title: Capital Planning and Space Management (Planning and Budget)
text: The Capital Financial Plan (CFP) is updated annually and represents an ongoing, collaborative planning effort at both the campus and UC-wide levels. The CFP presents a proposed six-year budget and schedule for all capital projects exceeding $10,000,000.; As needed, Capital Planning staff prepare analyses of established capital priorities and assessments of campus space needs. These materials, along with new project proposals from academic deans, the University Librarian, the Vice Chancellor for the Division of Finance, Operations, and Administration (FOA), and others, are reviewed by the Chancellor, Campus Provost and Executive Vice Chancellor, and other campus leadership.; The CFP is submitted to the UC Office of the President as part of the Consolidated Capital Report where revisions may be negotiated as necessary. The plan is subsequently forwarded to the Regents and is formally accepted at their November meeting each year.; Capital projects;

title: Capital Planning and Space Management (Planning and Budget)
text: A capital project includes new construction, expansion, renovation, or replacement of existing facilities. Once a project cost exceeds $35,000 total project cost, approval is required from the Chancellor, VC FOA, or UC Regents, depending on scope and total project budget. Capital and Space Planning supports project sponsors throughout this approval process. To request a new capital project refer to the Start-A-Project process.; Capital Projects Flowchart; capital projects flowchart; Budget & Resource Management; Budget and Capital Resources; Capital & Space Planning; Start-A-Project Process; Space Requests; Space Management Principles; Last Modified: Apr 20, 2026;

title: Career Advice Network
text: HomeCareer; Aerial photo of campus, Monterey Bay and Santa Cruz.; Professional networks and services; UC Santa Cruz is dedicated to supporting the professional success of all of our alumni. We support a variety of professional networks and services. Browse below for webinars, online communities, courses, career fairs, and more!; If there is a resource you’d like to see that isn’t listed, please contact us at alumni@ucsc.edu.; North coast near UCSC.; UC Santa Cruz Career Success; We recognize that the need for career support doesn’t end after graduation. The UC Santa Cruz Career Success office provides alumni with continued access to online tools at no cost.; All alumni have access to job listings on Handshake, free participation in workshops and job fairs, and online resources such as Forage (job simulations) and Big Interview (interview prep, resume, and job-search curriculum).

title: Career Advice Network
text: Alumni within two years of graduation also receive access to additional digital platforms, including GoinGlobal (international job and internship listings) and Focus2 (assessments). In addition, they may schedule one-on-one coaching appointments covering topics such as job search strategies, résumé reviews, graduate school applications, interview preparation, and career decision-making.; Visit Career Success; Student and professor in science lab.; Post a job for students and fellow alumni; Calling all Banana Slug recruiters, hiring managers, and business owners! One of the best ways you can support your alumni network is by hiring students and other alumni. Here’s how:; Create an employer account on Handshake, UC Santa Cruz’s platform for connecting students and young alumni with job and internship opportunities.; Use the #SlugsHireSlugs hashtag to boost visibility when posting job openings on social media.;

title: Career Advice Network
text: Join our Career Success Partner Program to enhance your recruiting efforts and engage more deeply with top UC Santa Cruz talent.; Get Started with Handshake; Attend virtual career development webinars; Whether you’re exploring your next move or sharpening the skills you already have, our virtual career development programs are here to help you grow. Join live, online workshops and webinars led by industry professionals and career experts.; See upcoming webinars; Career Design Program; Unclear on your next career move? This program was built for that.; The UCSC Career Design Program is a 30-day, collaborative online experience that helps you get clear on what’s next and take action—alongside a supportive group of fellow alumni.; Whether you’re seeking more purpose, better pay, or simply a path that fits your life, this program gives you the structure, tools, and community to move forward with confidence.;

title: Career Advice Network
text: The spring cohort will begin on May 30, 2026. Enrollment is now open! ; Learn more & enroll; iGrad: Financial Wellness; iGrad is a finance companion designed to empower UC Santa Cruz students, alumni, faculty, and staff on their financial journey. Whether you’re aiming to budget smarter, explore scholarships, or plan for retirement, iGrad is your go-to platform for building financial wellness. Begin with a quick financial checkup to receive tailored recommendations, then delve into trending content to stay informed on the latest financial insights. Learn at your own pace with extensive resources, covering popular topics like budgeting, job searching, and networking. iGrad prioritizes your privacy with no data mining or advertising—just pure financial education.; Explore iGrad; COOP Careers; COOP Careers digital training program offers upskilling in three career tracks: Digital Marketing, Data Analytics, or Financial Services.

title: Career Advice Network
text: Available to alumni within 10 years of graduating, our workshops and holistic support model prepare you to break into the tech industry and launch a successful career.; COOP’s alumni-led approach aids low-income, first-generation college graduates in securing jobs paying $50,000 or more. The program is 100% free, providing certifications, mental health resources, and mindfulness training. Participants benefit from professional network connections, virtual and hybrid opportunities, ongoing employment support, and corporate learning; Learn with COOP; Career Coach Directory; Working with a Career Coach can accelerate your career growth and guide you toward greater fulfillment, no matter what stage you’re at or what role you hold. A UC Santa Cruz alum as your coach can uniquely help you leverage the distinctive education you received at UCSC—they understand better than anyone the strengths you bring to the table.; Explore Career Coaches; UC Alumni Career Network;

title: Career Advice Network
text: The UC Alumni Career Network is a high-impact, online series designed to provide UC alumni and community members with the insights, information, and connections to launch, grow and expand your career opportunities.; Start networking; UC Santa Cruz has over 137,000 Banana Slugs on LinkedIn, with over 13,000 members in the official alumni group. This is a great resource to explore what other Banana Slugs are doing and build your professional network. ; Join the UCSC Alumni Group; Career Library; Share Your Path. Inspire a Slug.; Career Library is a social impact platform helping students explore career paths through short videos from professionals like you. By sharing a 2–3 minute story of how you got into your field, you can help current students connect their interests to real careers.; It’s a simple way to give back, mentor, and inspire—one story at a time.; UC Santa Cruz Silicon Valley Campus; UCSC Silicon Valley Extension courses;

title: Career Advice Network
text: UCSC Silicon Valley Extension offers a gateway to cutting-edge courses taught by industry experts actively working in the field. Whether you’re looking to upgrade your skills, venture into new territories, in pursuit of accreditation or exam preparation, or simply a lifelong learner eager to study alongside industry leaders in the heart of Silicon Valley, you’re invited to expand your horizons, stay ahead of industry trends, and grow your professional network. ; Alumni receive 10% off any course valued at $500 or more.; UCSC Silicon Valley Extension Promo; Browse extension courses; Slugs @ Work; This initiative is a social event tailored for UCSC alumni employed at companies with a significant concentration of fellow Slugs. The program is designed to foster a strong alumni community within your company by co-hosting casual, happy hour-style networking mixers.;

title: Cars, University
text: We offer daily rentals for staff and student groups, vehicle lease-to-own arrangements, and advice to departments looking to purchase and operate a vehicle at the University. Our “Central Garage” also provides fueling, maintenance, and repair services for a growing campus. Presently, we are researching new technologies that will allow the campus fleet to be powered by sustainably produced energy sources in an effort to take the fleet into the 21st century. title: Cashier’s Office
text: The domestic rate (United States) is 2.95% or $3.00 (whichever is greater). Only transactions over $20 are subject to a $3.00 minimum.; The international rate is 4.25% or $3.00 (whichever is greater). Only transactions over $20 are subject to a $3.00 minimum.; The fee will be displayed to the user before they submit their payment and the user must acknowledge the total payment amount before they can continue.; The convenience fees are being charged by the third-party vendor (SmartPay/Transact) and are not collected by UC Santa Cruz.; In Person; Payments may be made on campus via our 24-hour depository located outside the North Entrance of the building. Payments can be made via cash, check, cashier’s check, or money order and are processed Monday through Friday;

title: Cashier’s Office
text: Payments placed in the depository after 8:00 AM will not be posted until the next business day. If you need to pay with a credit card, please pay via the UCSC eBill/ePay site.; Operating Hours; Monday–Friday: 1:00 p.m. to 4:00 p.m.; Location: Financial Service Center, 102 Hahn Student Services.; Via Mail; Payments via mail can be made with check, cashiers check, or money order. title: Center for Agroecology and Sustainable Food Systems
text: Rooted in the Division of Social Sciences at UC Santa Cruz, the Center for Agroecology is an organization made up of staff, faculty, and students who work to advance agroecology and equitable food systems through experiential education, participatory research, agricultural extension, and public service.; years of leadership in agroecology and sustainable agriculture; 32,300+; pounds of produce grown and distributed each year by students for Basic Needs on campus; 3.4 mil+; learners engaged online and at our sites; Learn more in our 2024-25 Impact Report; Expanding our impact through a transformative land stewardship opportunity; UC Santa Cruz and The Conservation Fund are partnering to acquire more than 400 acres of land, including 214 acres of coastal farmland to be managed by the Center for Agroecology.;

title: Center for Agroecology and Sustainable Food Systems
text: Building on our legacy as a pioneer of the organic movement, the certified organic land will offer a living laboratory for climate-resilient farming and restoration practices; Learn more about this opportunity; new coastal property; Through agroecological research, initiatives to increase food security and support local farms and food businesses, and educational opportunities, we seek to empower students, farmers, scientists, and the community to advance equitable, sustainable food systems.; a hand holding a seedling; Participatory research; people with farm equipment; Experiential education; people with beets in a field; Food systems initiatives; Our facilities; The Center for Agroecology manages the certified-organic UCSC Farm and Alan Chadwick Garden on the UC Santa Cruz campus, both internationally known sites for training and research in organic horticulture and agriculture. These facilities are available to students, researchers, and community members.;

title: Center for Agroecology and Sustainable Food Systems
text: The Center also manages the historic Cowell Ranch Hay Barn, which serves as office space for staff and a community event space, and the Cowell Coffee Shop, a non-transactional cafe designed to address student food insecurity.; Learn more about our facilities; Two women lean over irrigation sensor technology; Grants support DIY irrigation and smart, electric tractors for specialty crop growers; Three students sit on the "porter squiggle" sculpture to watch the sunset; Nine major wins worth celebrating for 2025; Sheyna Burns; UC Santa Cruz student Sheyna Burns awarded state grant to build pathways for equitable food systems; More news; UCSC Farm U-Pick; July 25, 2026; Free Guided Tour of the UCSC Farm; August 1, 2026; Wildlife Cameras and Other Methods for Assessing Backyard Biodiversity; August 2, 2026; People holding broccoli; Support our work;

title: Center for Cultural Studies
text: The Center for Cultural Studies at UC Santa Cruz was was founded in 1988 as part of the University of California President’s Humanities Initiative. Since then, it has built a reputation as a bastion of cutting-edge interdisciplinary thinking on pressing social, political, and environmental issues. Through colloquia, invited lectures, workshops, conferences, and other forums, the Center continues to facilitate a broad range of research in the international field of cultural studies, and to contribute to an atmosphere of rigorous scholarly engagement across the boundaries of conventional fields and disciplines. It remains a major incubator for multidisciplinary and cross-divisional activity on campus and beyond.; Search our Site; Sign up for our newsletter by sending an email to: cult@ucsc.edu; The Humanities Institute Logo; Supported by The Humanities Institute. © 2018. title: Center for Molecular Biology of RNA
text: UCSC RNA Center; Illustration of DNA strand; One of the largest communities of RNA scientists in the world is at the University of California at Santa Cruz —biologists, chemists, geneticists, engineers, and computer scientists — and their work is defining the future of this field.; UCSC RNA Center News; Harry Noller leaning on a parapet; RNA Center founder Harry Noller cements a legacy of brilliance and creativity; The visionary who discovered that RNA powers the ribosome now turns his focus to his other lifelong loves: his saxophone, vintage cars, and family.; Sperm cell entering an egg cell; Tiny RNA molecules in sperm, big impact on baby health; Molecular mechanism uncovered in mice by UC Santa Cruz researchers reveals how a father’s diet, stress, and other environmental factors before conception can influence his offspring’s health; UC Santa Cruz Professor William Scott on a bike on a dirt road; In Memoriam: William Scott;

title: Center for Molecular Biology of RNA
text: The longtime professor of chemistry and biochemistry is remembered by family, friends, and colleagues for his immense intellect, generosity, and contributions to advance scientific research; Read more news; Scientists in the UC Santa Cruz Center for Molecular Biology of RNA are pushing the boundaries of the central dogma of molecular biology — the theory that explains how genetic information flows from DNA to RNA to produce proteins and regulate other functions in living cells. ; This is just the beginning. Discoveries are made every day — the field is exploding and attracting interest among many early-career scientists, public and private funders, and biotech companies. This is, in part, due to the deployment of exciting new computational tools and methods that were undreamed of only a few years ago, some of which were invented at UC Santa Cruz. ;

title: Center for Molecular Biology of RNA
text: As the science of RNA biology continues to explode, the Center for Molecular Biology of RNA at UCSC is poised to play a major role in solving the mysteries of this ancient molecule, and forging exciting new approaches to the understanding, diagnosis, and treatment of human disease.” ; — Harry Noller, Professor Emeritus of Molecular, Cell and Developmental Biology;; Robert L. Sinsheimer Professor of Molecular Biology; Director, Center for Molecular Biology of RNA; 350; Affliated scientist worldwide; Research groups at UCSC; Infinite possibilities; Last modified: Apr 11, 2026;

title: Center for Research in Open Source Software (CROSS)
text: Bridging the gap between student research and open source projects, the Center for Research in Open Source Software (CROSS) provides funding to work on innovative projects after graduation. CROSS facilitates a smart way to finance open source software, and is conscious of the abilities students have to create innovative software and encourages this innovation after graduation. UCSC created CROSS as a center that assists students with their research prototypes to collaborate with open source communities and companies.; OSPO logo; UCSC OSPO; CROSS and UCSC OSPO team works with University of California Open Source Program Office Network!; The UC OSPO Network is a groundbreaking initiative that harnesses the collective power of six UC campuses to revolutionize open-source practices in academia. This collaborative effort aims to amplify the impact of research, public service, and education across the University of California system.; UCSC OSPO ↗; CROSS News; Inaugural PyTorch Santa Cruz Meetup;

title: Center for Tropical Research in Ecology, Agriculture, and Development (CenTREAD)
text: Primary Menu; Visiting Scholars; Courses; External Funding; Research Grants; Collaborations; Welcome to the Center for Tropical Research in Ecology, Agriculture, and Development!; The goal of the Center for Tropical Research in Ecology, Agriculture, and Development (CenTREAD) is to foster the interdisciplinary research and training needed to understand tropical environmental issues and develop ecologically-based, economically viable, culturally respectful, nonexploitative solutions that serve as a foundation for future generations. We are a coalition of faculty and students that span a number of departments and centers on the University of California, Santa Cruz campus. CenTREAD members are involved in a wide range of natural science and social science research on tropical environmental issues, including understanding the roles of biodiversity and food web relationships in tropical forests, studying social movements and the environment, and combining cultural and agroecological analyses in land-use management systems.;

title: Center for Tropical Research in Ecology, Agriculture, and Development (CenTREAD)
text: CenTREAD strives to provide training and research support for environmental scientists, land managers, and rural development specialists in tropical countries. We works towards this goal by:; •Providing seed grants for tropical research to graduate students from various UCSC departments; •Sponsoring interdisciplinary working groups; •Supporting graduate exchange programs and short courses for students from tropical regions; •Hosting international visiting scholars to UCSC.; Facebook LinkedIn Twitter Instagram; Search for:; Search …; Text Widget; This is a text widget, which allows you to add text or HTML to your sidebar. title: Chicano/Latino Research Center (CLRC)
text: Scenes from the Archive; Oral Histories; Nuestras Memorias; Meet the Collaborators; Nuestras Historias: The CLRC Archive Project; Nuestras Historias is the 2015-16 iteration of the CLRC's Undergraduate Research Apprenticeship Program. This project preserves the legacy of the CLRC (renamed as the Dolores Huerta Research Center for the Americas in 2022) and makes a wealth of historical materials dating back to our founding in 1992 publicly available. With these goals in mind, a group of undergraduate researchers established the CLRC archive by collecting oral histories of the CLRC's founders and earliest directors and by building a digital repository. Commemorating the CLRC's 25th anniversary, Nuestras Historias culminated with a public exhibition launch at McHenry Library on Thursday, November 30, 2017.; The Finding Aid to the archives is available at the Online Archive of California website as well as via PDF.;

title: Chicano Latino Student Life Resource Center – El Centro
text: The Chicanx Latinx Resource Center is part of a network of campus resource centers that work together to support all students. In collaboration with centers like AARCC, AIRC, AA/PIRC, Lionel Cantú Queer Center and Women’s Center, we offer shared programs, resources, and spaces that celebrate identity, build community, and promote belonging. In addition to exploring our offerings, we encourage you to check out the many events, services, and opportunities provided by our partner centers across campus. UCSC resource centers are open to all students, and everyone is welcome.;

title: Child Care
text: The Early Education Center is dedicated to providing the highest quality developmental care possible in a secure, homelike environment characterized by warmth, affection, and support. We believe that children learn through play and exploration while engaged in relationships with peers and responsive, caring adults. We nurture all aspects of a child’s development: cognitive, emotional, social, creative, and physical.; Learn about our programs;

Our primary goal is to report and analyze issues affecting the student population and the Santa Cruz community.; We also serve to watchdog the politics of the UC administration. While we endeavor to present multiple sides of a story, we realize our own outlooks influence the presentation of the news. The City on a Hill Press (CHP) collective is dedicated to covering underreported events, ideas and voices. Our desks are devoted to certain topics: campus and city news, sports, arts and entertainment, opinion and editorial.

title: City on a Hill Press
text: CHP is a campus paper, but it also provides space for Santa Cruz residents to present their views and interact with the campus community. Ideally, CHP’s pages will serve as an arena for debate, challenge, and ultimately, change.; CHP is published weekly in the fall, winter and spring quarters by the City on a Hill Press publishing group, except during Thanksgiving and academic breaks.; The opinions expressed in this paper do not necessarily reflect the opinions of the staff at large, or the University of California.; Search here...; WordPress Theme built by Shufflehound.;

title: College Nine
text: UCSC College Nine Aerial View; College Nine, founded in 2000, is centered on the theme of International and Global Perspectives. The college helps students develop cultural awareness and leadership skills through academic and co-curricular opportunities. Students join a welcoming community committed to global understanding, collaboration, and positive change.; UCSC College Nine Campus Life; Student Opportunities; College Nine offers leadership, scholarships, programs, and creative outlets—from student government and academic funding to the Garden Club and mural projects—giving students ways to build skills, make connections, and shape their college experience.; UCSC College Nine Campus Life - Quarry Plaza; College Courses; Courses offered through College Nine are interdisciplinary and closely connected to our unique theme and community. They’re designed to help students build academic skills, explore new ideas, and prepare for success in college and beyond.; UCSC College Nine Campus Life - UCSC Sign; Events & Activities;

title: College Nine

title: Colleges, UCSC
text: Students sharing their Slug Stories; Slug Stories: A College Nine and John R. Lewis College student storytelling tradition; Slug Stories is an annual storytelling event hosted by College Nine and John R. Lewis College, where first-year students take the stage to share personal narratives that reflect their identities, challenges, and growth. The event is the culmination of a yearlong initiative that integrates storytelling into the Core Course, building connection, empathy, and community through live story slams, a digital archive, and creative exploration.; UCSC mascot, Sammy, celebrating Pride; Kresge presents PRIDE: A student-led march and festival for LGBTQIA+ community; Kresge Presents PRIDE is UC Santa Cruz’s largest annual queer gathering—a student-led march and festival that celebrates LGBTQIA+ identity, visibility, and community.

title: Colleges, UCSC
text: The event has grown into a favorite campus tradition, bringing students together for a cross-campus march, affinity spaces, and a lively festival of music, food, and resources. title: Commencement Information
text: Graduates throwing their caps into the air.; Commencement 2026; How is commencement different from graduation?; Great question! Graduation is the official acknowledgment that a degree is completed and recorded. Commencement is a ceremony—you know, the one with the caps and gowns!—where friends, family, faculty, and staff celebrate the graduates and take a moment to honor this tremendous accomplishment.; Explore this site and get all the details about commencement.; Friday, June 12 to Monday, June 15 | Upper East Field; 9 a.m. – Stevenson; 1 p.m. – Cowell; 5 p.m. – Crown & Merrill; Saturday, June 13; 9 a.m. – Porter; 1 p.m. – Kresge; 5 p.m. – College Nine & John R. Lewis; Sunday, June 14; 9 a.m. – Oakes; 1 p.m. – Rachel Carson;

No matter where you are, you’re always a Banana Slug.;

title: Community Safety Program
text: HomeLiving at UCSCSafety and Security; Walkway outside on campus; Safety and security in university housing; UC Santa Cruz is committed to creating a safer and supportive environment where you can learn, grow, and thrive. This page highlights the many programs, people, and tools that help keep our residential communities safer. From our on-campus safety teams to late-night ride services and emergency alerts, these resources are here to support your well-being around the clock. ; Community safety teams; RCSP logo; Residential Community Service Partners (RCSPs); RCSPs are dedicated campus staff who help create a safer and supportive environment in our residential communities at night. Trained in emergency response, first aid, CPR, and disaster response, you can find them in campus housing areas between 7 p.m. and 3 a.m., checking that everything is running smoothly and responding to concerns.

title: Community Safety Program
text: RCSPs work closely with Santa Cruz Fire, University Police, and campus partners, and carry radios linked to University Dispatch to coordinate help when it’s needed.; They can assist with:; Lockouts and securing buildings; First aid; Noise and quality of life concerns; Documenting policy violations; Nighttime maintenance issues; Emergency calls (fire, medical, or crisis); Residential event security; RCSPs are approachable and ready to help—if you see one in your community, you’re always welcome to say hello or ask for assistance. You can also reach their Dispatch line at (831) 459-2100.; To provide feedback about the Residential Community Service Program, please submit a feedback form, or reach the program office at (831) 459-4692. All comments, questions, and concerns are welcome.; UCSC police dispatch; UCSC Police Department ;

title: Community Safety Program
text: Our campus police officers have full municipal authority and practice community-oriented policing—engaging with students, staff, and faculty to build trust and support safety on campus 24/7.; Emergencies: Dial 9-1-1 or use a Blue Light phone (see below).; Non-emergencies: (831) 459-2231 ext. 1.; Learn more about UCSC Police; Campus Mobile Crisis Team next to van; Campus Mobile Crisis Team (CMCT); An extension of Counseling & Psychological Services (CAPS), the Campus Mobile Crisis Team brings culturally competent behavioral health support to students in distress:; Wellness checks & crisis intervention; Coordination with CAPS referrals; De-escalation & mental health first response; Contact CMCT: (831) 502-9988; Learn more about CMCT; Quick safety tips every Slug should know;

title: Community Safety Program
text: Lock your doors and windows—even if you’re just stepping out or hanging out inside.; Don’t prop open exterior doors or let in people you don’t know.; Stay aware of your surroundings, especially at night (keep earbud volume low and your phone away).; Don’t tamper with smoke detectors or sprinklers—and never hang anything from them.; Know your exits—review your building’s evacuation map.; Emergency alerts & phones; Blue Light Emergency Phones; Installed in high-traffic areas, these phones connect you directly to Public Safety Dispatch. The blue light turns on automatically after dark; just pick up the handset to call for help.; Find blue light phone locations; Campus alerts: CruzAlert & SlugSafe; UC Santa Cruz uses two official notification systems to keep our community informed:;

title: Community Safety Program
text: CruzAlert is used for emergencies that involve an immediate threat to health or safety—like natural disasters or active threats. Alerts go out by text, phone, and email as soon as a situation is confirmed. All UCSC students, faculty, and staff are automatically enrolled.; SlugSafe provides non-emergency updates about campus safety, operations, or disruptions—like road closures, power outages, or weather advisories.; Learn more about campus alerts; Additional campus safety and security resources; UCSC Safe Ride van; After-hours transportation: Safe Ride; Safe Ride offers free, student-operated rides across the residential campus from 7 p.m. to midnight, seven days a week (when classes are in session). Request a ride the same day using the Flexigo app. Drivers are fellow UCSC students who are fully trained, background checked, and certified in defensive driving.; Learn more about Safe Ride; someone performing maintenance and repairs;

title: Community Safety Program
text: Emergency maintenance: CruzFix; If you notice a safety- or security-related repair—like flooding, a broken entry lock, or loss of power—that can’t wait until the next business day, call our CruzFix helplines right away:; Monday–Friday, 8 a.m.–5 p.m.: (831) 459-7043; After hours, weekends & holidays: (831) 459-4861; For all other non-urgent maintenance needs, please submit a standard service request through CruzFix.; Learn more about CruzFix; Fire safety and prevention; UCSC has a Campus Fire Station. The Fire Prevention Division of the Office of Emergency Services makes it a priority to educate campus staff, faculty, and students to reduce fires and injuries on campus and routinely gives presentations to campus members.; Learn more about UCSC fire safety and prevention; Emergency preparedness;

title: Community Safety Program
text: The Office of Emergency Management offers resources and training to help students prepare for emergencies—from campus evacuation plans and safety videos to volunteer programs and CPR training. Learn how to stay informed, stay ready, and take action when it matters most.; Reporting hate and bias; UC Santa Cruz has zero tolerance for any form of hate or bias. We have a reporting structure in place to report hate or bias, and a Hate/Bias Response Team.; Need immediate support?; Whether it’s a mental health crisis, safety concern, or urgent basic needs, help is available—day or night. Visit our Get Urgent Help page to find the resources you need, when you need them.; Last modified: Oct 27, 2025;

title: Community Studies Department
text: students at habitat for humanity site; The Community Studies Program teaches effective techniques and strategies for community organizing and social movement work and provides extensive real-world learning experiences to prepare students for careers spent making a difference on social justice issues.; If you have a vision for a type of change you’d like to see in the world, we’re here to help you make it a reality.; Earth Day student with sign; Community Studies major; A Bachelor of Arts degree in community studies is ideal for those who want to lead social change work. We help students develop a strong understanding of the systems and power dynamics that contribute to society’s problems and what it takes to disrupt them. All of our students also embark on six months of field study, a uniquely rewarding form of learning that allows you to make a difference for others while gaining valuable real-world experience.; Learn more about the major;

title: Community Studies Department
text: Faculty in the Community Studies Program have historically pioneered approaches to experiential learning and social justice that were central to UC Santa Cruz’s founding ethos and have continued to define the campus’s identity and goals. Today, our core and affiliated faculty continue to innovate, while drawing upon our deep history.; Together, we are a community of activist-scholars and esteemed educators dedicated to mentoring students who are passionate about community activism and public service.; Meet our faculty; student with faculty at poster session; union organizers marching; Our affiliated faculty have research specialties in more than a dozen topics related to social and environmental justice. Through equitable and inclusive research practices, we co-create knowledge alongside the communities we serve. ; Our research seeks to answer big questions about inequity, stigma, bias, and discrimination in our society, using techniques like critical analysis, ethnography, and applied social science methodology.; Community Studies campus news;

title: Community Studies Department

title: Copier Program
text: UCSC Copier Program; A person reading on Porter College's Wave sculpture; The UCSC Copier Program…; provides high-quality, self-serve multifunctional devices to the campus community on a cost-per-copy basis. These devices are set up for access control and account management with Pharos Uniprint®, or as sole source copiers with no access control. We place Ricoh and Canon MFDs that offer black and white and/or color output (copy/print/fax), and all new devices are equipped as scanners. Other features are available upon request. For more information about the Copier Program have a look around our website, and feel free to call or email us with any questions or needs you may have: 831-459-5799, or copierprogram@ucsc.edu.;

title: Copier Program
text: The Copier Program offers campus units state-of-the-art multifunctional devices and delivers exceptional customer service in support of UCSC students, staff, and faculty. We aim to keep our fleet as current as possible, our prices as low as possible, and our customers as happy as possible. In doing so we support UCSC’s mission to enable students to succeed while here at UCSC, and to prepare them for the challenges of the future.; As our customers’ satisfaction reflects the Copier Program’s success, we pride ourselves on bringing attention, empathy, and value to everything we do for our customers. The Copier Program seeks to become recognized as the benchmark of outstanding customer service across all of UCSC.; Our Community; As part of the UCSC campus community, the Copier Program shares core values that include a commitment to diversity, social responsibility, and environmental stewardship.

title: Copier Program
text: Together we promote and protect a community that values and supports every person in an atmosphere of civility, honesty, cooperation, professionalism, and fairness. The UC Santa Cruz Principles of Community guide our daily interactions on campus and beyond.; Department News; Bill Kasper is retiring…finally!; After 31 years on campus, and 15 as manager of the Copier Program, on July 1, 2026 Bill will be leaving gainful employment and moving into the life of a retiree. He started in 1995 as the Copier Program assistant, when there were only 40 Copier Program devices; as he leaves we have well over 175 devices in units across campus and beyond. Annika will be taking over full day-to-day operations, while Doug Lang will (at least temporarily) act as Copier Program Manager. Best of luck, Bill!; Pharos Servers Moving: AD to AU;

title: Copier Program
text: As we have seen in many operations over the past 18 months ITS is requiring Copier Program to move off of the ad.ucsc.edu domain; we are moving onto au.ucsc.edu. The Copier Program servers that support Pharos must move in mid-November, 2025. Watch this space for expected impacts and proposed solutions.; We Have a New Program Assistant!; In March, 2025, Annika Estrada joined Bill at the Copier Program. A recent graduate of UCLA, Annika has an outgoing personality matched with a quick wit, and both her computing and customer service backgrounds mean she is a serious asset to the program. We’re excited to have her onboard! Be sure to say hi when you see her around campus during her day-to-day operational duties.; Goodbye Baskin Engineering, Hello H Barn; After more than 30 years living in the basement of Baskin Engineering, the Copier Program is moving!

title: Copier Program
text: Our new space will be in the old Risk Management space in the H Barn at the base of campus…if you’re facing Receiving/Surplus on the loading dock we’ll be in the suite of offices on the left end of the loading dock. We will move during the week of November 12, 2024; during that week, unfortunately, we will be even less available than we have been while we’re understaffed. Thank you for your patience and understanding!; Pharos 9.2 Upgrade Completed!; Over Spring Break we successfully upgraded Pharos to version 9.2, which will allow us to place more advanced Canons on campus for Pharos users. Once we figure out how to do that so we can help people with issues found (there will be issues!) we will bring new Canons to campus to replace old devices that are past time for replacement. Thanks for your patience!; What used to be News;

title: Copy Services
text: UCSC Copy Center; Image of trees and sustainabile practices.; The UCSC Copy Center…is MOVING!; We move from Baskin Engineering room B25 to H Barn room 102 (behind the TAPS Sales Office) in July, 2026. PLEASE PLAN AHEAD FOR YOUR SUMMER SESSION MATERIALS!; Our last day in Baskin will be Friday, June 26, 2026. We will reopen on Monday, August 3, 2026.; Our last day for job submission will be Wednesday, June 24, 2026. If we can’t fulfill the job prior to Friday, June 26, we will have to refuse the job (though we’ll be glad to delay fulfillment until August!).; All finished jobs need to be picked up by June 26, 2026, or they will be moved to H Barn and available after we reopen on August 3, 2026.;

title: Copy Services
text: The UCSC Copy Center provides the UCSC campus community high-quality, cost-effective printed output of all sizes and types. Our staff collaborates with you to assist with all phases of your print requests, from graphic design to delivery. Whether you want one copy of a directional sign, multiple copies of a booket, printed tablecloths, or enough addressed “Welcome” postcards to greet the entire Freshman class, the Copy Center has got you covered! We offer complete Professor Publishing services, and, since 2021, sells Course Readers directly to students (rather than through the Bookstore).; For more information about the Copy Center have a look around our website, and feel free to call or email us with any questions or needs you may have: 831-459-3888, or email us. Thank you!; Our Vision and Values;

title: Copy Services
text: After 31 years on campus, and 7 as manager of the Copy Center, on July 1, 2026 Bill will be leaving gainful employment and moving into the life of a retiree. He started in 1995 as the Copier Program assistant, and began managing the Copy Center in 2019. The rest of the crew will continue their excellent work in the H Barn when they reopen in August, with Doug Lang acting as temporary manager. Best of luck, Bill!; We have a new Production Assistant!; In March, 2025, Gabe Donohoe joined Cary and company at the Copy Center. A recent resident of Alaska, Gabe brings a wealth of production knowledge, and a bright, personable demeanor. He doesn’t seem to get ruffled easily, and is always looking for ways to help you with your printing project. We’re excited to have him at the Copy Center!

title: Copy Services
text: Be sure to say hello when you come to drop off or pick up your job(s) at the Copy Center.; Welcome Nick! We finally added our new team member, Nick Leoncito, to be our outbound mail and LiveScan Services coordinator in April, 2024. Nick’s a quick learner, and his humor and intelligence (and background in computing, shipping, and customer service)will hold him in good stead as he learns the peculiar ways of the University. We’re happy to have him at the Copy Center; you’ll get to meet him on your first day on campus (LiveScan) or any time you drop off a package.; Last modified: Jun 25, 2026;

title: Cowell College
text: UC Santa Cruz, Cowell College campus life; Founded in 1965 as a bold experiment in reimagining higher education, Cowell College is the first residential college established at UC Santa Cruz. It is home to the historic Cowell Press, the Eloise Pickard Smith Gallery, and a hands-on core course focused on justice and ethics. Guided by the motto “The pursuit of truth in the company of friends,” Cowell offers students a place to think deeply, act creatively, and build a supportive, engaged community.; News & Updates; From student highlights and campus events to college traditions and community stories, find out what’s happening at Cowell College. Stay connected with the latest updates, memorable moments, and stories that showcase our vibrant community.; Cowell Programs; Cowell College is home to a variety of academic, artistic, and community-centered programs that reflect its commitment to intellectual exploration, public service, and creative expression.;

title: Cowell College
text: UC Santa Cruz, Cowell College from above; Cowell students can enjoy a full range of events from open mic nights to talent shows, College Nights, and volunteer opportunities. Programming is student-centered, and Cowell welcomes your ideas and energy in shaping community life.; cowell@ucsc.edu; Stay connected for updates, events, and campus highlights. title: Crime Awareness & Campus Security Report
text: UC Santa Cruz Police Department; HomeCrime PreventionCrime Visualization Dashboard; It is recommended to view this dashboard on a computer or display with a wide screen.; Open dashboard in a new page; Citizen RIMS Interactive Crime Log and Map; Citizen RIMS Interactive Map; Open crime log and map in a new page; Our goal is to provide the community with information about safety issues so that they may take appropriate and timely measures to take care of themselves and others. As such, we have created a website that offers the community the opportunity to view reports of crime and incidents, missing people, stolen vehicles, and more on a map of campus. One may also sign up for daily activity reports that will automatically be sent to an email account.; This website also gives users the option to narrow a search by date (up to 30 days in the past) and type of crime report. Users can also see crime reports as pushpins or a heat map.;

title: Crime Awareness & Campus Security Report
text: This website pulls the media log, Clery Act crime and fire log, as well as the other pertinent information into one place. The Clery Act log will include information for the past 60 days. Moreover, each October, as part of our requirements under state and federal law, we release the Annual Campus Security and Fire Safety Report, which provides crime statistics as well as information on safety and security prevention and protection programs and much more. In addition, the University of California publishes a crime and security report throughout the UC system for several calendar years.; Crime Dashboard; Community Alerts; Traffic Safety; Road Safety Tips; Scams; Public Service Videos; Security Systems; Cruz Alert; Safe Party Initiative; Last modified: Nov 03, 2025;

title: Critical Race and Ethnic Studies
text: Critical Race & Ethnic Studies; Imagine a more just world; Born of decades of student struggle and faculty advocacy, Critical Race and Ethnic Studies (CRES) is grounded in critique of the dominant forces of power and repression. At a time of growing fascism, inequality, militarization and war, CRES provides essential analyses of the social, political, cultural, and economic processes at play. Our programs prepare students to be informed and engaged citizens, ready to address the problems of the modern world.; Professor Nick Mitchell teaching CRES 10; CRES Bachelor of Arts; The CRES major offers a study of race as a major ideological framework through which practices of power and domination as well as struggles for liberation and self-determination have been enacted throughout history and in the contemporary moment.; Professors and student presenters after the John R. Lewis college lightning talk in 2022; Black Studies Minor;

title: Critical Race and Ethnic Studies
text: The Black Studies minor offers students grounding in the intellectual histories, political movements, cultural expressions, and critical theories of the black diaspora, all while engaging a range of methodologies from across disciplines.; Professor Xavier Livermon teaching a grad class; CRES Graduate Designated Emphasis; The designated emphasis (DE) is the equivalent of a “minor” for doctoral study. It will provide you with a framework for in-depth study in specialized fields in your area, and an opportunity for recognition of particular scholarly expertise.; Advising Services; CRES advisors are here to support you on your educational journey.; Learn more about advising services; Advisor Dejon Barber advising a student; Research Emphases; Student walking near mural that features a fox, crane, and turtles that reads "we do not inherit the earth from our parents, we borrow it from our children"; Creative CRES;

title: Critical Race and Ethnic Studies
text: Creative CRES is an area of research, cultural production, arts practice, teaching, and social engagement that generatively blurs the distinction between “creative” and “critical” work. Stemming from our commitment to justice as a method, Creative CRES highlights the importance of arts and literary practice to struggles for racial justice.; Professors Sophia Azeb and Jenny Kelly speaking with two students outside of Humanities 1 building.; Settler Colonialism/Empire; A through line across multiple areas of study, our faculty study how settler colonialism – the ongoing genocidal theft of Indigenous peoples’ land across the globe – is a structural aspect of racial capitalism. In CRES, we bring together Indigenous Studies, Latinx Studies, and Black Studies to trace the centrality of settler colonialism within the accumulation of profit as predicated on colonial exploitation: racialization, elimination, enslavement and incarceration.; Greenhouse project entrance; Science Studies;

title: Critical Race and Ethnic Studies
text: By centering justice, science studies in CRES builds communities of knowledge-making that engage with queer, Indigenous, and liberatory worldviews, and indeed interrogates the relationship of technoscience to capitalism as a system that requires and produces inequality. A Science and Justice minor is currently under development.; and much more!; Visit our faculty’s profile pages to read more about their research.; Faculty Profiles; Humanities skills are transferable skills; UC Santa Cruz humanities students graduate with a toolkit of transferable skills in high demand across fields ranging from academia to the entertainment industry and high-tech startups. Here are the stories of UC Santa Cruz humanities graduates thriving in a wide range of jobs.; Humanities EXCEL Fellows build community through hands-on Internships; At UC Santa Cruz, the Humanities EXCEL Program is helping Humanities undergraduates discover new pathways to professional growth and connect to local communities through paid internships. ; November 17, 2025;

title: Critical Race and Ethnic Studies
text: Humanistic approaches to urgent environmental issues ; At UC Santa Cruz, the environmental humanities encourage us to ask: what happens when we stop treating the natural world as merely a backdrop, resource, or object of study—and instead treat it as a web of interconnected lives?; November 10, 2025; Community Statements; Follow the link below for an archive of CRES department community statements.; Read Our Statements; Text "Black Lives Matter" painted on a street; additional information:; CRES Instagram; Last modified: Jun 22, 2026;

In embracing this diversity, we find stimulation, freedom, and strength.
